attributes.stem: A 56-year-old woman undergoes an emergency right hemicolectomy for an obstructing carcinoma of the caecum. She has a BMI of 32 and a 30 pack-year smoking history. She does not take any medication. The surgery is uneventful and she makes a good post-operative recovery, apart from a chest infection. Six days after the operation a serosanguinous discharge is observed from the lower end of the wound.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
A serosanguinous discharge appearing from an abdominal wound several days after surgery is almost diagnostic of a deep wound dehiscence. In this case, the main contributing risk factors for the wound dehiscence include the woman's BMI >30, her smoking and emergency surgery. Other risk factors for surgical wound dehiscence include old age, surgical site infection, history of COPD and chronic inflammatory conditions. In general, a high BMI is associated with a significantly increased risk of many adverse post-operative outcomes of surgery, particularly abdominal procedures. Smoking is a well-known risk factor for post-operative wound healing complications. The management of wound dehiscence would include urgent revision laparotomy and exploration for closure of the abdominal fascia. If closure is not possible, negative pressure wound therapy should be used.

Purulent discharge is characteristically seen from a wound infection, where the discharge is less likely to be serosanguinous. Other cardinal signs of a localised infection including erythema, pain, and warmth at the surgical site. A haematoma is a result of a collection of blood due to failure of haemostasis, which may manifest as abdominal pain and swelling at the site locally and can result in an infection.

Whilst an anastomotic leak usually presents 5-7 days post-operatively, such a patient would usually be unwell with fever and abdominal pain that may be peritonitic.

Necrotising fasciitis is a life-threatening complication of cellulitis and is a rare complication of surgery. These patients are usually systemically unwell and septic. There may be crepitus around the wound margins and a wound discharge is not usually part of the process.
learningPoints: Serosanguinous discharge is characteristic of wound dehiscence, whereas purulent discharge should alert a sign of infection.
references: Walming, S., Angenete, E., Block, M. et al. Retrospective review of risk factors for surgical wound dehiscence and incisional hernia. BMC Surg 17, 19 (2017). https://doi.org/10.1186/s12893-017-0207-0

Jensen, K.K., Oma, E., van Ramshorst, G.H. et al. Abdominal wound dehiscence is dangerous: a nationwide study of 14,169 patients undergoing elective open resection for colonic cancer. Hernia 26, 75–86 (2022). https://doi.org/10.1007/s10029-020-02350-z
